titleStmt.title: Two-band Model with High Thermoelectric Power Factor and Its Application to FeSe Thin Film

affiliation.orgName: Research Institute for Science and Technology
Research Institute for Science and Technology

analytic.title: Two-band Model with High Thermoelectric Power Factor and Its Application to FeSe Thin Film

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We propose a simple theoretical model referred to as the two-band model to realize both a large Seebeck coefficient and high electrical conductivity, resulting in a high thermoelectric (TE) power factor (PF). Using the Kubo-Luttinger linear response theory, we apply this model to the TE response of an FeSe thin film reported by Shimizu et al. to show a high PF with strong temperature dependence. The effects of superconducting fluctuations and excitonic correlations are found to not be critical.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Because of recent societal needs for energy harvesting technology, the development of high-performance thermoelectric (TE) materials has been a topic of intense interest. The performance of a TE material is characterized by its power factor PF = L 11 S 2 and the dimensionless figure of merit ZT = (PF/ΈΜ)T ; the former represents the maximum output, and the latter represents the maximum efficiency. Because the PF and ZT are determined by three intercorrelated physical quantities (i.e., the electrical conductivity L 11 , the Seebeck coefficient S , and the thermal conductivity ΈΜ), enhancing the performance of TE materials is difficult. Nevertheless, extensive effort has been devoted to developing materials with enhanced PF and ZT values, and several high-performance TE materials with ZT > 2 have recently been reported. 1) In addition, new TE materials with high PF values (e.g., organic materials 2) and carbon nanotubes 3) ) have also recently been developed. To attain TE materials with even higher performance, we propose a theoretical model with a high PF, which is made possible by modulating electronic states.

In principle, the PF can be large even for a moderate S if the L 11 is sufficiently high in systems with many valleys with degeneracy, n v , because L 11 is proportional to n v , whereas S is independent of n v . This is not easy to realize, however, and then efforts to find systems with a large (absolute value of) S have been pursued. [4][5][6] Because S is dependent on the details of a material's electronic state, collaborative theoreticalexperimental studies through searches for new materials, experimental characterization of their electronic properties, and detailed theoretical analysis are strongly desired. (This situation is similar to efforts to realize superconductors with a high critical temperature, T c .) In pursuing this cycle, we expect that focusing on known cases with particular features and then analyzing them in detail to extract essential properties of the electronic states underlying the experimental facts will guide further efforts to develop new materials. In the present paper, we propose a simple theoretical model, the two-band model, to realize both a large S and high L 11 , leading to a high PF.

We then apply it to recent experimental data for an FeSe thin film with a high PF, as reported by Shimizu et al. 7) Shimizu et al. have reported that an FeSe thin film shows an unprecedented large PF value in the presence of a ver-* mmatsubara@rs.tus.ac.jp Here, m H/L is the effective mass of the higher/lower-band electrons.
div.formula: E β³è 2 k 2 2m L k Έî β³è 2 k 2 2m H + Έî 0 (a) E 0 DOS m L 2œÄβ³è 2 m L + m H 2œÄβ³è 2 Έî (b)
div.p: tical electric field E βäΞ over a wide temperature (T ) range (50 K < T < 300 K). 7) The FeSe thin film exhibits strongly metallic character. Its L 11 increases with decreasing T below room T , with the L 11 βàΦ 4 Ο½ 10 6 S/m at T βàΦ 100 K and the film eventually becoming superconducting below T c βàΦ 50 K. By contrast, S shows weak T dependences for 50 K < T < 200 K, with a large value of 350 ¬ΒV/K, leading to a PF of βàΦ 500 mW/(mβÄΔK 2 ) at T βàΦ 100 K. 7) Bulk FeSe consists of two-dimensional FeSe layers and is known to be a semimetal. 7,8) The electron and hole energy bands at the Fermi energy consist mainly of the d orbitals of Fe, and both bands are doubly degenerate. Similar to bulk FeSe, monolayer FeSe is also a semimetal. 9) Such a semimetal does not usually show a large S similar to that of a semiconductor. Thus, we speculate that the large S of FeSe thin films originates from the effects of the E βäΞ applied to thin films. The E βäΞ would not only open the bandgap of FeSe but also split its degenerate energy bands (Fig. 1). In fact, Shimizu et al. have reported an experimental investigation of the fieldinduced bandgap of FeSe thin films. 7) In the present paper, we show that the two-band model achieves a high PF. We then analyze the experimental data for an FeSe thin film on the basis of the proposed model. The TE response functions are calculated according to Kubo-Luttinger (KL) theory combined with the Green's function method, which is a fully quantum theory for TE transport in materials. 10,11)

div.head: Model and Theory
div.p: In this section, we explain the KL theory ( ¬ß 2.1) and introduce the two-band model ( ¬ß 2.2).

div.head: Kubo-Luttinger theory of TE response
div.p: We explain the KL theory combined with the Green's function method, which enables the TE response to be analyzed fully quantum mechanically. 10,11) The current density J in the presence of both an electric field E and a temperature gradient dT/dx in the x-direction in the material is described as
div.formula: J = L 11 E - L 12 T dT dx (1)
div.p: within the linear response to E and dT/dx. Here, L 11 and L 12 are the electrical conductivity and the thermoelectrical conductivity, respectively. Using L 11 and L 12 , we can express S as
and can describe the PF by
According to the KL theory, L 11 and L 12 are given by
div.formula: L 11 βàù Έ≤ 0 dœ³ T œ³ {J el (œ³)J el (0)} e iœâ ΈΜ œ³ ,(4)
L 12 βàù Έ≤ 0 dœ³ T œ³ {J el (œ³)J th (0)} e iœâ ΈΜ œ³ ,(5)
div.p: where J el and J th are electrical and thermal current operators, respectively. On the basis of the KL theory together with the method introduced by Jonson and Mahan, 12) Ogata and Fukuyama 13) clarified that L 11 and L 12 can be expressed as the Sommerfeld-Bethe relation, 14)
div.formula: L 11 = βàû -βàû dE - βà² f (E -¬Β) βà²E Έ±(E),(6)
L 12 = - 1 e βàû -βàû dE - βà² f (E -¬Β) βà²E (E -¬Β)Έ±(E),(7)
div.p: even for strongly disordered systems (with particular types of both mutual electron interactions and electron-phonon interactions). [15][16][17][18][19] Here, Έ±(E) is the spectral conductivity, e is the elementary charge, ¬Β is the chemical potential, and
is the Fermi-Dirac distribution function.

div.head: Two-band model for an FeSe thin film 2.2.1 Effective Hamiltonian
div.p: We introduce a two-band model in two dimensions that is applicable as an effective Hamiltonian for an FeSe thin film in E βäΞ . The Hamiltonian of the band model is given by
div.formula: H = g s g o k «Ϊ L (k)c βÄ† Lk c Lk + «Ϊ H (k)c βÄ† Hk c Hk ,(8)
div.p: where
div.formula: «Ϊ L = 2 k 2 2m L and «Ϊ H = 2 k 2
div.p: 2m H + βàÜ are the energy dispersions for the lower and higher conduction bands, respectively, with an energy spacing βàÜ (Fig. 1(a)). Here, c βÄ† Lk and c βÄ† Hk (c Lk and c Hk ) are the creation (annihilation) operators for the lower and -0.5 0 0.5 1.0 1.5
div.formula: E/Έî ψÉΦ 50K ψÉΦ 100K ψÉΦ 200K ψÉΦ 300K
div.p: DOS (arb. unit) Here, βàÜ is the energy spacing between the higher-and lower-band bottoms.

higher conduction-band electrons with the two-dimensional wavenumber k = (k x , k y ). m L and m H are the effective masses of the lower and higher conduction-band electrons, respectively. g s and g o are the spin and orbital degrees of freedom, respectively. For an FeSe thin film, they are given by g s = 2 and g o = 1. The origin of energy is taken to be the bottom of the lower conduction band.

div.head: Green's function
div.p: We introduce a retarded Green's function as
div.formula: G R L/H (E, k) = 1 E -«Ϊ L/H -ΈΘ R L/H ,(9)
div.p: where ΈΘ R L/H is the retarded self-energy due to several scattering events for the lower/higher conduction-band electrons. In the present study, we adopt the constant-œ³ approximation with respect to energy E and wavenumber k as
div.formula: ΈΘ R L/H = -i 2œ³ L/H(10)
div.p: and consider electron-phonon interaction and impurity scattering as scattering processes in the system. /œ³ L/H is assumed to be
div.formula: œ³ L/H (T ) = A L/H T 5 + B L/H ,(11)
div.p: where the first term with parameter A L/H is the contribution from the electron-phonon scattering; the Bloch-GrΟΦneisen law (the T 5 -law) is assumed, and the second term with the parameter B L/H is the contribution from the impurity scattering, which is independent of T .

div.head: Electronic state: Density of states
div.p: The density of states (DOS) œ¹(E) per unit area in the presence of scattering for the two-band model is expressed as in terms of the retarded Green's function. Here, β³Π is the system area. Substituting G R L/H (E, k) in Eqs. ( 9) and ( 10) into Eq. ( 12), we can analytically calculate œ¹(E) as
div.formula: œ¹(E) = - g s g o œÄβ³Π k ImG R L (E, k) + ImG R H (E, k)(12)
œ¹(E) = g s g o 2œÄ 2 2 {m L (œÄ -ΈΗ L ) + m H (œÄ -ΈΗ H )} ,(13)
div.p: where ΈΗ L/H are defined in terms of the relaxation times œ³ L/H ,
div.formula: ΈΗ L = arctan 2œ³ L E (0 βâΛ ΈΗ L < œÄ),(14)
ΈΗ H = arctan 2œ³ H (E -βàÜ) (0 βâΛ ΈΗ H < œÄ).(15)
div.p: Here, the DOS in Eq. ( 13) is effective one which depends on T through œ³ L/H in Eq. (11). Figure 2 shows œ¹(E) of the two-band model at several temperatures for the choice of m L = m H = 2.7m 0 (m 0 : electron mass in vacuum), βàÜ = 29 meV, A L = 1.0 Ο½ 10 -13 eV/K 5 , A H = 2 Ο½ 10 -15 eV/K 5 , and B L = B H = 0. Notably, these parameters are determined to reproduce the experimental data for an FeSe thin film, as will be shown later ( ¬ß 3.1). The solid blue, orange, green, and red curves correspond to œ¹(E) at T = 50, 100, 200, and 300 K, respectively. The blue curve (T = 50 K) has a stepwise structure with a sharp step reflecting the two-dimensionality of the present system. As T increases, the stepwise DOS become smoother because œ³ L/H become short and eventually ΈΗ L/H in Eqs. ( 14) and ( 15) become large. In Fig. 2, compared with the higher-band bottom, the lower-band bottom has a greater scattering effect on the DOS.
The spectral conductivity Έ±(E) is described in terms of the Green's functions as
div.formula: Έ±(E) = g s g o e 2 œÄV k v 2 L ImG R L (E, k) 2 + v 2 H ImG R H (E, k) 2 ,(16)
div.p: where v L/H = k x /m L/H is the group velocity in the xdirection and V is the system volume. The summation with respect to k can be analytically performed; Έ±(E) is eventually given by
div.formula: Έ±(E) = g s g o e 2 4œÄ 2 d 2œ³ L E(œÄ -ΈΗ L ) + 1 + 2œ³ H (E -βàÜ)(œÄ -ΈΗ H ) + 1 ,(17)
div.p: where d = 4 ΟÖ is the distance between the atomic layers in the system. Here, we note that Έ±(E) is independent of the effective masses m H and m L , which is a characteristic feature of two-dimensional electron systems. The results indicate that the present system is a good metal and that the electron-phonon scattering is dominant in comparison with the impurity scattering within the present T region. By contrast, A H < A L indicates that the lower-band electrons are more affected by electronphonon scattering than the higher-band electrons. For reference, A = 5 Ο½ 10 -13 eV/K 5 and B = 3 Ο½ 10 -5 eV for copper. 20) If the two bands are assumed to be degenerate, then the best-fit parameters are ¬Β = 0.1 meV and A L = A H = 0.4Ο½10 -13 eV/K 5 for B L = B H = 0 eV, as in the two-band model. The results show that the experimental data cannot be reproduced by the degenerate two-band model with βàÜ = 0 eV. As we will explain in the next section, to realize both a large S and high L 11 , it is critical that the chemical potential (¬Β = 0.1 meV ) be located between the higher and lower conduction bands and that A H be smaller than A L . By contrast, the experimental data in Ref. 7 could not be reproduced under the condition of A H = A L for any βàÜ.

div.head: Temperature dependence of S and PF
div.p: By substituting the calculated L 11 and L 12 values into Eqs. ( 2) and (3), we can obtain the S and PF values. the Azlamasov-Larkin process 21,22) and the Maki-Thompson process 23,24) -have been proposed theoretically and identified experimentally in thin Al films. 25) The Azlamasov-Larkin process is responsible for the main contributions near T c , which is given as follows by the conductivity œÉ βÄ≤ arising from the fluctuation for a thin film with thickness d:
div.formula: œÉ βÄ≤ = e 2 œÄ d œÄ16
T c T -T c . (18
div.p: This result is to be compared with the dominant contributions to conductivity, œÉ 0 , given by
div.formula: œÉ 0 = e 2 œÄ d œ³E .(19)
div.p: Here, œÉ 0 can be determined from the experimental data 7) (e.g., œ³E/ = 5 Ο½ 10 11 T -5 ); thus, œÉ βÄ≤ is smaller than œÉ 0 by an order of /œ³E in the T range except around T c , which then leads to small contributions to L 11 . The contribution of œÉ βÄ≤ to L 12 is also small because œÉ βÄ≤ is weakly dependent on energy in the present case. This fact has been numerically assessed (e.g., the weak T dependence of S with a large value of 350 ¬ΒV/K for 50 K < T < 300 K is difficult to understand in terms of superconducting fluctuations).
div.formula: E g =10meV E g =10meV Έî =5meV Έî =10meV Έî =0meV L L L Έî =5meV Έî =10meV Έî =0meV L L L

div.head: Effects of excitonic correlations
div.p: The system of present interest, FeSe, is semimetallic with an equal number of electrons and holes when in bulk form; however, in thin-film form, FeSe has more electrons under an applied gate voltage. In such a situation, effects of excitonic correlations, 26) including orbital ordering, 27) are reasonably expected. To assess these effects, we incorporated the valence band into the two-band model via the following 3 Ο½ 3 Hamiltonian:
div.formula: H(k) = οΘΪ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ≠ 2 k 2 2m + E g 2 + βàÜ 0 βàÜ H ex 0 2 k 2 2m + E g 2 βàÜ L ex βàÜ H ex βàÜ L ex - 2 k 2 2m - E g 2 οΘΕ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΗ .(20)
div.p: Here, E g is the energy gap between the lower conduction band and the valence band, βàÜ is the energy spacing between the bottoms of the lower and higher conduction bands, and βàÜ L/H ex is the excitonic order parameter for the lower/higher conduction band. βàÜ L/H ex is assumed to be expressed as follows by the mean-field solution in mind: where T 0 is the critical temperature. In the following calculation, we set m = 2.7m 0 , E g = 10 meV, βàÜ = 29 meV, and T 0 = 200 K. We also adopted the constant-œ³ approximation to the self-energy, as in Eq. (10). (See the Appendix for the calculation procedure of the TE response in this system; the calculation is based on the KL theory within the constant-œ³ approximation.)
div.formula: βàÜ L/H ex = βàÜ L/H tanh οΘΪ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ≠ T 0 -T T 0 οΘΕ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΗ ,(21)
E g =10meV E g =10meV Έî =5meV Έî =10meV Έî =0meV L L L Έî =5meV Έî =10meV Έî =0meV L L L
div.p: We now discuss the two special cases of (i) βàÜ H ex = 0 and (ii) βàÜ L ex = 0 to understand possible effects of excitonic correlations on the lower and higher bands separately.

(i) Case of βàÜ H ex = 0 Figure 6 shows the energy dependence of the DOS and the spectral conductivity at T = T 0 /2=100 K for several values of βàÜ L . In this calculation, the relaxation times are chosen to be /œ³ v = /œ³ L = 1 meV and /œ³ H = 0.02 meV. As βàÜ L increases, the energy gap between the lower band and the valence band becomes large and the DOS increases near the bottom of the lower band, as shown in Fig. 6(a). As a result, Έ±(E) in Fig. 6(b) decreases near the bottom of the lower band.

Figure 7 represents the ¬Β dependence of S and PF at T = 100 K for several values of βàÜ L . S increases slightly with increasing βàÜ L when ¬Β locates near the bottom of the lower band. However, the excitonic effect on PF is negligibly small even near the lower-band bottom. (ii) Case of βàÜ L ex = 0 Figure 8 shows the DOS and Έ±(E) at T = 100 K for several values of βàÜ H . In this calculation, the relaxation times are set as /œ³ v = /œ³ H = 0.02 meV and /œ³ L = 1 meV. Figure 8(a) shows that excitonic effects appear near the bottom of the higher band but are weaker than in the case of βàÜ H ex = 0. Similarly, excitonic effects on Έ±(E) are also small, as shown in Fig. 8(b). Because of the small change of Έ±(E), the values of S and PF are weakly affected by excitonic interaction.
div.formula: E g =10meV H H H Έî =5meV Έî =10meV Έî =0meV H H H E g =10meV

div.head: Summary
div.p: We introduced a simple theoretical model, the two-band model, to attain both a large S and high L 11 and thereby obtain a high PF and then applied it to an FeSe thin film under E βäΞ . We succeeded in understanding the recent experimental report by Shimizu et al., 7) who found that an FeSe thin film in the presence of E βäΞ exhibits a high PF originating from a large S and high L 11 . In the case of an FeSe thin film, ¬Β locates near the bottom of the lower conduction band, resulting in S βàΦ 350 ¬ΒV/K and PF βàΦ 500 mW/(mβÄΔK 2 ) at T βàΦ 100 K. We also examined the effects of superconducting fluctuations and excitonic correlations, and we conclude that their effects are not critical for the high PF of an FeSe thin film.

Similar candidates with a high PF resulting from the two bands are nanowires and nanotubes with nanosized diameters because their energy bands have sub-band structures as a result of quantum confinement effects along the diameter direction. Notably, the sub-band gap in these candidates is independent of E βäΞ and only ¬Β can be controlled by tuning E βäΞ , in contrast to FeSe thin films.

Appendix: Excitonic effects on the DOS and the spectral conductivity In this Appendix, we represent analytical expressions of the DOS and the spectral conductivity for the two-band model incorporating the effects of excitonic correction, which is described by the Hamiltonian in Eq. ( 20). In the following discussion, we focus on the case of βàÜ H ex = 0, as discussed in the main text.
We adopt the constant-œ³ approximation for the self-energy as
div.formula: ΈΘ R/A = οΘΪ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ≠ βà™i /2œ³ H 0 0 0 βà™i /2œ³ L 0 0 0 βà™i /2œ³ v οΘΕ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΗ , (AβÄΔ1)
div.p: where we set œ³ L = œ³ v . The DOS is described as
div.formula: œ¹(E) = -g s g o 1 œÄβ³Π Tr οΘΪ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ§ οΘ≠ k ImG R (E, k) οΘΕ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΖ οΘΗ (AβÄΔ2)
div.p: in terms of the retarded Green's function G R (E, k) = EI -H(k) -ΈΘ R -1 and can be analytically calculated as
div.formula: œ¹(E) = œ¹ H (E) + œ¹ L-v (E) (AβÄΔ3) with œ¹ H (E) = g s g o m 2œÄ 2 2 (œÄ -Έ‰ H ) (AβÄΔ4)
div.p: and
div.formula: œ¹ L-v (E) = g s g o m 2œÄ 2 2 Im οΘ° οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘΑ |E| + i 2œ³ L z R οΘ± οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ≤ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ≥ ln E g 2 + z R E g 2 -z R +i(œÄ -Έ‰ (-)
div.p: L-v + Έ‰ (+) L-v ) . (AβÄΔ5) Here, œ¹ H (E) is the DOS of the higher band without excitonic correlations and œ¹ L-v (E) is the DOS of the lower conduction band and the valence band including excitonic correlations. z R/A are given by
div.formula: z R/A = (|E| ¬± i /2œ³ L ) 2 -βàÜ L ex 2 (AβÄΔ6)
div.p: with Im z R βâΞ 0 and Im z A βâΛ 0. Έ‰ H and Έ‰ (¬±) L-v are defined as
div.formula: Έ‰ H = arctan 2œ³ H E - E g 2 -βàÜ (0 βâΛ Έ‰ H < œÄ), (AβÄΔ7) Έ‰ (¬±) L-v = arctan Im z R Re z R ¬± E g 2 (0 βâΛ Έ‰ (¬±) L-v < œÄ). (AβÄΔ8)
div.p: The spectral conductivity Έ±(E) is expressed as
div.formula: Έ±(E) = g s g o e 2 2œÄV k Re Tr v x G A (E, k)v x G R (E, k) -v x G R (E, k)v x G R (E, k) (AβÄΔ9)
div.p: in terms of the retarded/advanced Green's functions G R/A (E, k) and the velocity matrix v x = -1 βà²H(k)/βà²k x in the x-direction along the temperature gradient of the system. Within the constant-œ³ approximation, Eq. (AβÄΔ9) can be calculated as
div.formula: Έ±(E) = Έ± H (E) + Έ± L-v (E) (AβÄΔ10)
div.p: with the contribution from the higher conduction band,
div.formula: Έ± H (E) = g s g o e 2 4œÄ 2 d 2œ³ H E - E g 2 -βàÜ (œÄ -Έ‰ H ) + 1 (AβÄΔ11)
div.p: and the contribution from both the lower conduction band and the valence band,
div.formula: Έ± L-v (E) = g s g o e 2 4œÄ 2 d 2œ³ L |E| E 2 -βàÜ L ex 2 œÄ -Έ‰ (-) L-v -Έ‰ (+) L-v - E g 2 Im οΘ° οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘ· οΘΑ E 2 -βàÜ L ex 2 + i 2œ³ L |E| z R οΘ± οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ≤ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ≥ ln E g 2 + z R E g 2 -z R + i œÄ -Έ‰ (-) L-v + Έ‰ (+) L-v οΘΦ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘΫ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘΨ οΘΙ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΜ οΘΦ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘΫ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘ¥ οΘΨ + 2 οΘΙ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΚ οΘΜ . (AβÄΔ12)
div.p: The DOS and the spectral conductivity for the case of βàÜ L ex = 0 are obtained similarly.

figure.head: Fig. 1 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1. (Color online) (a) Higher and lower energy bands with energy spacing βàÜ. (b) Density of states (DOS) for the two-band model in two dimensions.Here, m H/L is the effective mass of the higher/lower-band electrons.

figure.head: Fig. 2 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 2. (Color online) Effective density of states in the presence of scattering for the two-band model for several temperatures: T = 50, 100, 200, and 300 K. Here, βàÜ is the energy spacing between the higher-and lower-band bottoms.

figure.head: Fig. 3 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 3. (Color online) Spectral conductivity Έ± for several temperatures: T = 50, 100, 200, and 300 K. The inset denotes the T dependences of Έ± for several fixed energies: E/βàÜ = 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 with βàÜ = 29 meV.

figure.head: Fig. 4 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 4. (Color online) T dependences of (a) L 11 and (b) L 12 for an FeSe thin film. The solid circles are experimental data in Ref. 7. The solid curve represents the theoretical curve calculated on the basis of the two-band model with the energy spacing βàÜ = 29 meV. The dashed curve denotes the theoretical curve calculated on the basis of the degenerate two-band model with βàÜ = 0 eV.

figure.head: Figure 3
figure.figDesc: shows Έ±(E) for several temperatures: T = 50, 100, 200, and 300 K. Έ±(E) increases stepwise with increasing E, reflecting the DOS. The inset in Fig.3denotes the T dependences of Έ±(E) for several fixed energies: E/βàÜ = 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0. The figure shows that Έ±(E) with a fixed E increases almost exponentially as T decreases.3. Simulation Results3.1 Temperature dependence of L11 and L 12 Figures 4(a) and 4(b) represent the T dependence of L 11 and L 12 of the two-band model from 50 K to 300 K, along with the experimental data for an FeSe thin film, where T c = 50 K is the superconducting transition temperature of the FeSe thin film. The solid circles are experimental data in the case of finite gate voltage V G = 3.95 V reported by Shimizu et al. 7) L 11 and |L 12 | decay strongly with increasing T because of the electron-phonon scattering. The solid curves in Figs. 4(a) and 4(b) are theoretical curves calculated on the basis of the twoband model with a finite split. The parameters in the model are determined by fitting the experimental data: βàÜ = 29 meV, ¬Β = 0.1 meV, A L = 1.0 Ο½ 10 -13 eV/K 5 , A H = 2 Ο½ 10 -15 eV/K 5 , and B L = B H = 0 eV.

figure.head: Fig. 5 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 5. (Color online) T dependences of the (a) PF and (b) S in the twoband mode, along with the experimental data for an FeSe thin film. The solid circles are experimental data reported in Ref. 7. The solid curve represents the theoretical curve calculated on the basis of the two-band model with the energy spacing βàÜ = 29 meV. The dashed curve denotes the theoretical curve calculated on the basis of the degenerate two-band model with βàÜ = 0 eV.

figure.head: Fig. 6 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 6. (Color online) Energy dependence of (a) the DOS and (b) the spectral conductivity of an FeSe thin film with a narrow gap E g = 10 meV for βàÜ L = 0 (dashed curve), 5 meV (red curve), and 10 meV (blue curve) at T = 100 K. βàÜ H ex is zero for all curves.

figure.head: Fig. 7 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 7. (Color online) The ¬Β dependence of (a) S and (b) PF of an FeSe thin film with a narrow gap E g = 10 meV for βàÜ L = 0 (dashed curve), 5 meV (red curve), and 10 meV (blue curve) at T = 100 K. βàÜ H ex is zero for all curves.

figure.head: Fig. 8 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 8. (Color online) Energy dependence of (a) the DOS and (b) the spectral conductivity of an FeSe thin film with a narrow gap (E g = 10 meV)for βàÜ H = 0 (dashed curve), 5 meV (red curve), and 10 meV (blue curve) at T = 100 K. βàÜ L ex is zero for all the curves.

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: We thank Yoshihiro Iwasa for the fruitful discussions on experiments on FeSe thin films. This work was partly supported by JSPS KAKENHI (Grant No. 22K18954).
